We will consider systems of more than one congruence that involve the same number of unknowns and have the same modulus.

Find all integers \(x\) and \(y\) such that both of the congruences:

$$\begin{gather} 3x + 4y ≡ 5 \bmod 13 \\ 2x + 5y ≡ 7 \bmod 13 \end{gather}$$

are satisfied.

We can rewrite the congruences as:

$$\begin{gather} 15x + 20y ≡ 25 \bmod 13 \\ 8x + 20y ≡ 28 \bmod 13 \end{gather}$$

Subtracting one from the other:

$$\begin{gather} 7x ≡ -3 \bmod 13 \end{gather}$$

Since \((7,13)=1\), then exists an inverse of 7, which by manually checking is 2:

$$\begin{gather} 2*7x ≡ 2*-3 \bmod 13 \\ 14x ≡ -6 \bmod 13 \\ x ≡ 7 \bmod 13 \end{gather}$$

Likewise, we can rewrite the congruences like this:

$$\begin{gather} 6x + 8y ≡ 10 \bmod 13 \\ 6x + 15y ≡ 21 \bmod 13\end{gather}$$

Subtracting one from the other:

$$\begin{gather} 7y ≡ 11 \bmod 13 \\ 2*7y ≡ 2*11 \bmod 13 \\ y ≡ 22 ≡ 9 \bmod 13 \end{gather}$$

We have shown that any solution \((x,y)\) must satisfy:

$$ x ≡ 7 \bmod 13 \\ y ≡ 9 \bmod 13$$

Find all integers \(x\) and \(y\) such that both of the congruences:

$$\begin{gather} x + 2y ≡ 1 \bmod 5 \\ 2x + y ≡ 1 \bmod 5 \end{gather}$$

are satisfied.

We can rewrite the congruences as:

$$\begin{gather} x + 2y ≡ 1 \bmod 5 \\ 4x + 2y ≡ 2 \bmod 5 \end{gather}$$

Subtracting one from the other:

$$ 3x ≡ 1 \bmod 5 $$

Since \((3,5)=1\), then exists an inverse of 3, which by manually checking is 2:

$$\begin{gather} 2*3x ≡ 2*1 \bmod 5 \\ 6x ≡ 2 \bmod 5 \\ x ≡ 2 \bmod 5 \end{gather}$$

Likewise, we can rewrite the congruences like this:

$$\begin{gather} 2x + 4y ≡ 2 \bmod 5 \\ 2x + y ≡ 1 \bmod 5 \end{gather}$$

Subtracting one from the other:

$$\begin{gather} 3y ≡ 1 \bmod 5 \\ 2*3y ≡ 2 \bmod 5 \\ 6y ≡ 2 \bmod 5 \\ y ≡ 2 \bmod 5 \end{gather}$$

We have shown that any solution \((x,y)\) must satisfy:

$$ x ≡ 2 \bmod 13 \\ y ≡ 2 \bmod 13$$

Find all integers \(x\) and \(y\) such that both of the congruences:

$$\begin{gather} x + 3y ≡ 1 \bmod 5 \\ 3x + 4y ≡ 2 \bmod 5 \end{gather}$$

are satisfied.

We can rewrite the congruences as:

$$\begin{gather} 3x + 9y ≡ 3 \bmod 5 \\ 3x + 4y ≡ 2 \bmod 5 \end{gather}$$

Subtracting one from the other:

$$ 5y ≡ 1 \bmod 5 $$

Since all multiples of 5 are congruent to 0 modulo 5, then no integer \(y\) can exists such \(5y ≡ 1 \bmod 5\). Therefore, a solution does not exist.

Find all integers \(x\) and \(y\) such that both of the congruences:

$$\begin{gather} 2x + 3y ≡ 5 \bmod 7 \\ x + 5y ≡ 6 \bmod 7 \end{gather}$$

are satisfied.

We can rewrite the congruences as:

$$\begin{gather} 2x + 3y ≡ 5 \bmod 7 \\ 2x + 10y ≡ 12 \bmod 7 \end{gather}$$

Subtracting one from the other:

$$ 7y ≡ 7 \bmod 7 $$

This means \(y\) can be any integer.

Likewise, we can rewrite the congruences like this:

$$\begin{gather} 10x + 15y ≡ 25 \bmod 7 \\ 3x + 15y ≡ 18 \bmod 7 \end{gather}$$

Subtracting one from the other:

$$\begin{gather} 7x ≡ 7 \bmod 7 \end{gather}$$

This means \(x\) any integer.
